# Introduction
The Markowitz framework, a cornerstone of modern portfolio theory, has been widely used for portfolio optimization [1]. However, it has several limitations [1]. One major issue is the instability of the allocation solutions, which increases with the number of assets, a phenomenon known as Markowitz's curse [3]. This curse is exacerbated in high-dimensional scenarios where the number of assets is of the order of the number of observations [5]. Furthermore, the Markowitz framework assumes that the covariance matrix is known a priori, which is not the case in real-world applications [2].

# Key Findings
The limitations of the Markowitz framework can be attributed to several factors, including the instability of the allocation solutions, the assumption of a known covariance matrix, and the concentration of investment weights on a few stocks [2]. The random matrix theory (RMT) has been proposed as a solution to improve the covariance matrix estimation involved in the portfolio optimization problem [3]. RMT can be used to control the correlation matrix, which can improve portfolio management [2]. Additionally, the Markowitz framework can be generalized to include structural changes in the financial markets, which can lead to co-movements within the financial markets [1].

The use of RMT techniques can help to mitigate the limitations of the Markowitz framework [3]. For example, RMT can be used to estimate the covariance matrix, which can improve the accuracy of the portfolio optimization problem [5]. Furthermore, RMT can be used to identify the underlying structure of the financial markets, which can help to improve the diversification of the portfolio [4]. The mean-variance allocation strategy of Markowitz can be modified to include constraints on the weights, which can help to reduce the risk of the portfolio [5].

# Methodological Considerations
The application of RMT techniques to the Markowitz framework requires careful consideration of the methodological aspects [3]. For example, the choice of the number of records to consider is crucial, as a highly correlated portfolio is common during systemic contagion events [1]. Additionally, the estimation of the covariance matrix is a critical step, as it can significantly impact the accuracy of the portfolio optimization problem [5]. The use of RMT techniques can help to improve the estimation of the covariance matrix, which can lead to more accurate portfolio optimization solutions [3].

# Gaps & Future Directions
